subject: Physics
solution:
Given:
Mass of the particle, $m = 5 \text{ mg} = 5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ kg}$
Charge, $q = 5\pi \times 10^{-6} \text{ C}$
Velocity, $\vec{v} = (3\hat{i} + 2\hat{k}) \times 10^{-2} \text{ m/s}$
Magnetic field, $\vec{B} = 0.1\hat{k} \text{ Wb/m}^2$

The velocity component parallel to the magnetic field is $v_{\parallel} = 2 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m/s}$.

The time period of one revolution is given by:
$T = \dfrac{2\pi m}{qB}$

Substituting the given values:
$T = \dfrac{2\pi \times 5 \times 10^{-6}}{5\pi \times 10^{-6} \times 0.1} = \dfrac{2}{0.1} = 20 \text{ s}$

The time taken to complete $5$ revolutions is:
$t = 5T = 5 \times 20 = 100 \text{ s}$

The distance moved along the $\hat{k}$ direction (pitch for $5$ revolutions) is:
$\alpha = v_{\parallel} \times t$
$\alpha = 2 \times 10^{-2} \times 100 = 2 \text{ m}$

Answer: $2$

subject: Physics
solution:
Magnetic field due to $\mathrm{AB} \& \mathrm{CD}=0$
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{B}_0=\left|\mathrm{B}_{\mathrm{R}_1}-\mathrm{B}_{\mathrm{R}_2}\right| \\ & =\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{I}}{4 \mathrm{R}_2}-\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{I}}{4 \mathrm{R}_1} \\ & =\frac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 12}{4}\left(\frac{1}{4 \pi}-\frac{1}{6 \pi}\right) \\ & =12 \pi \times 10^{-7}\left(\frac{1}{12 \pi}\right) \\ & =1 \times 10^{-7}
\end{aligned}$
$K=1$

solution:
When a charged particle moves in an electric field, the field applies an electric force F→=qE→ along the field, so work done by electric field is non-zero resulting in accelerated motion, thus kinetic energy changes.

When a particle moves in a uniform magnetic field, the force due to magnetic field on it is perpendicular to its velocity, as force  F→=qv→×B→.
Since the magnetic field is constant, so force is constant. So, its direction of velocity constantly changes, but magnitude of velocity remains same. Hence, kinetic energy also does not change.

 Electric field accelerates the particle in the direction of field F→=qE→=ma→and magnetic field accelerates the particle perpendicular to the field F→=qv→×B→=ma→.

$$
F=\frac{\mu_0}{2 \pi} \frac{l_1 l_2}{d} \ell
$$
According to question
$$
F^{\prime}=\frac{\mu_0}{2 \pi} \frac{\left(-2 I_1\right)\left(I_2\right)}{d} \ell
$$
From equation (i) and (ii), $\mathrm{F}^{\prime}=-\frac{3}{2} \mathrm{~F}$.

options: <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><msub><mrow><mi>F</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover><mi> </mi></math>  is radially inwards and  <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><msub><mrow><mi>F</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover><mi> </mi></math>  is radially outwards
solution:
S2 is a solenoid with more radius than S1, the magnetic field of the solenoid S2 will be along the axis and constant, so the magnetic force on an element of solenoid S1 will be,

dF→1=il→×B→

dF→1=ilBj^×-i^=iBlk^

Similarly, for just next coil the magnetic field is in same direction, but the current element is in opposite direction, so,

dF→1=ilB-j^×-i^=-iBlk^.

From Symmetry, we can say that the overall force will be cancelled to the whole coil, hence, F→1=0

Similarly, from coil S1 to S2, The magnetic field of the inner solenoid is symmetric about its axis, but not exactly along the axis of the outer solenoid. As the outer solenoid is placed symmetrically the push of any coil will be equal and opposite to the pull of coil placed exactly the opposite distance from the centre. Hence, the net force on the outer coil will be zero, i.e., F→2=0.

subject: Physics
solution:
Radius of a charged particle moving in a magnetic field perpendicular to its velocity is given by, R=mvqB.

mv can be written in terms of kinetic energy as mv=2mKE. Therefore,

R=mvqB=2mKEqB

⇒R∝KE⇒R2R1=K2K1=41=21

subject: Physics
solution:
For a magnetic dipole in an external field, torque is $\tau = mB\sin\theta$
At 30°: $0.016 = m \times 0.08 \times \sin 30°$
$0.016 = m \times 0.08 \times 0.5$
$m = 0.4$ A·m$^2$
Work done from most stable (aligned, 0°) to most unstable (anti-aligned, 180°) position equals the change in potential energy:
$W = U_0° - U_{180°} = -mB\cos 0° - (-mB\cos 180°) = -mB - mB = 2mB$
$W = 2 \times 0.4 \times 0.08 = 0.064$ J $= 64 \times 10^{-3}$ J
Therefore, $\alpha = 64$

subject: Physics
solution:
The force per unit length between parallel wires is $F/L = \frac{\mu_0 I_1 I_2}{2\pi d}$.
Force on Q from P (opposite currents, repulsive towards R): $\frac{F_{QP}}{L} = \frac{4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 3 \times 1}{2\pi \times 0.03} = 2 \times 10^{-5}$ N/m.
Force on Q from R (same currents, attractive towards R): $\frac{F_{QR}}{L} = \frac{4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 1 \times 2}{2\pi \times 0.02} = 2 \times 10^{-5}$ N/m.
Total force per unit length towards R: $4 \times 10^{-5}$ N/m.
For 0.15 m length: $F = 4 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.15 = 6 \times 10^{-6}$ N towards R.

solution:
$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}_0=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}_{\mathrm{AB}}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}_{\mathrm{DE}}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}_{\mathrm{BCD}}$
$=\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{i}}{4 \pi \mathrm{r}} \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{i}}{4 \pi \mathrm{r}} \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{i}}{2 \mathrm{r}} \hat{\mathrm{i}}$
$=\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{i}}{2 \pi \mathrm{r}} \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{i}}{2 \mathrm{r}} \hat{\mathrm{i}}$
$=\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{i}}{2 \pi \mathrm{r}}(1-\pi) \hat{\mathrm{i}}$
$=-\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{i}}{2 \pi \mathrm{r}}(\pi-1) \hat{\mathrm{i}}$

subject: Physics
solution:
Point $P$ is situated at the mid-point of the line joining the centres of the circular wires which have same radii (R). The magnetic fields $(\vec{B})$ at $\mathrm{P}$ due to the currents in the wires are in same direction.
Magnitude of magnetic field at point, $P$
$B=2\left\{\frac{\mu_0 N I R^2}{2\left(R^2+\frac{R^2}{4}\right)^{3 / 2}}\right\}$
$=\frac{\mu_0 N I R^2}{\frac{5^{3 / 2}}{8}}=\frac{8 \mu_0 N I}{5^{3 / 2} R}$

subject: Physics
solution:
(a) When the bar magnet entering the coil the magnetic flux link with the coil increases, and according to the Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction, the emf will induce in the coil, which in tern induces current and galvanometer shows the deflection towards right.

(b) When the bar magnet is inside the coil no change in magnetic flux link with the coil, therefore galvanometer do not show deflection.

(c) When the bar magnet exit from the coil the magnetic flux link with the coil decreases, and according to the Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction, the emf will induce in the coil, in opposite direction which in tern induces current and galvanometer shows the deflection towards left.
